name: Behavioral Experiment
tagline: Test an anxious prediction in real life instead of believing it.

origin.source: Cognitive Therapy (Beck et al., 1979); Bennett-Levy et al. (2004)

situations: A fear you've never actually tested
Avoiding something because of a 'what if'
Believing 'if I do X, something bad will happen'
summary: A behavioral experiment turns an anxious belief into a testable prediction, then runs the test in real life to see what actually happens. Instead of arguing with a fear in your head, you gather firsthand evidence. It's often the most powerful way to change a belief, because experience convinces where logic alone can't.
howItWorks: Anxiety thrives on untested predictions: avoidance prevents you from ever discovering the feared outcome usually doesn't occur, or that you'd cope if it did. By specifying the prediction, designing a safe test, and observing the real result, you replace imagined catastrophe with lived evidence. This experiential learning updates belief at a deeper level than verbal disputation.

steps.instruction: State the belief as a specific prediction: 'If I do X, then Y will happen.'
Rate how strongly you believe it and design a concrete, safe way to test it.
Run the experiment, dropping any safety behaviors that would cloud the result.
Compare what actually happened to your prediction, and re-rate the belief.
steps.note: Surprises are the goal — they're where learning lives.

whenToUse: When a fear has never actually been tested
To break an avoidance pattern with evidence
Checking a belief about how others will react
whenNotToUse: For genuinely dangerous predictions — don't test real risks
Without a fair, safe test design
In acute crisis or without support for high-stakes fears
tips: Make the prediction specific and measurable
Drop safety behaviors or they'll skew the result
Start with a low-stakes experiment to build confidence
Write down the result while it's fresh

evidence.summary: Behavioral experiments draw on exposure and cognitive change, both core to CBT, which has strong meta-analytic support for anxiety disorders.
citations: Bennett-Levy, J. et al. (2004). Oxford Guide to Behavioural Experiments in CBT. Oxford University Press.
